issues_found: Critical temporal leakage from stratified random split: The most serious flaw in this design is splitting 24 months of session data by random stratification rather than by time. Search sessions are not i.i.d. — a user's future behavior is informed by past behavior, product catalogs evolve, query trends shift, and the production ranker generating the logged impressions changes over time. Randomly assigning sessions to train/val/test means the model trains on sessions from December 2023 and is evaluated on sessions from January 2022, violating the causal ordering of information. This leaks future knowledge into training and produces optimistically biased offline metrics that will not replicate in production.

Click context feature leaks future session information under random split: The 'recent click/purchase context' feature encodes items clicked earlier in the same session. If sessions are split randomly across time, the model may be trained on later portions of sessions whose earlier portions appear in the test set, or vice versa. Even setting aside cross-session temporal leakage, the design does not clearly specify how session-level temporal ordering is preserved when constructing click context vectors, raising a risk that clicks occurring after the current ranking event contaminate the context signal.

Exposure bias and selection bias in logged impressions are underaddressed: The impression set is determined by the incumbent production ranker, meaning lower-ranked items are systematically underexposed. The design applies IPS correction to NDCG but uses raw, uncorrected clicks as the primary relevance labels for training and for the primary metric (per-item AUROC). Items the production ranker never showed users have no labels at all and are excluded. This means both models are evaluated only on the production ranker's candidate shortlist, making it impossible to detect whether the transformer would surface genuinely relevant items the incumbent never surfaced — a core claimed advantage of semantic encoding.

Baseline is deliberately handicapped in a way that conflates architecture with feature access: The design explicitly gives BM25 only query text while the transformer receives query, title, and click context, then frames this as testing whether 'contextual signals provide value.' This is true, but it means a positive result cannot be attributed to the transformer architecture — it could be explained entirely by the additional input signals. A fair architectural test would require a non-transformer model (e.g., gradient boosted trees or a shallow MLP) that also receives click context and product features. The current design tests 'transformer plus context vs. BM25 alone,' not 'transformer architecture vs. alternative architecture given equivalent inputs.'

Per-item AUROC is a poor primary metric for a ranking task and misaligns with the stated hypothesis: AUROC measures discrimination between clicked and unclicked items in aggregate but is insensitive to rank ordering within the relevant set and to the position of relevant items near the top of the list. The hypothesis is about ranking quality in e-commerce search, where the value of surfacing a relevant item decays steeply with rank position. Metrics like NDCG@10 or MRR@10 directly measure this; AUROC does not. Using AUROC as the primary selection and reporting metric optimizes for a property that does not directly correspond to the business objective. It is also used as the hyperparameter selection criterion, meaning both models are tuned toward a metric that is only loosely related to user experience.

Human annotation sample is drawn from the test set queries, creating an evaluation integrity risk: The design collects human judgments on approximately 5,000 queries sampled from the test set. If the same annotated subset is used both for calibration/confound detection (as described) and for reporting secondary metrics, this subset is no longer a clean held-out evaluation — it has received additional analytical attention that could, even inadvertently, influence how results are interpreted or reported. The annotation process itself could influence downstream decisions about which metrics to emphasize. The design should pre-register which queries will be annotated and treat that subset as a fixed secondary evaluation set with no feedback loop to model selection.

IPS propensity scores are computed from training sessions but applied to test sessions drawn from the same random sample: Because the split is not temporal, propensity scores estimated from training sessions (which include sessions from all 24 months) are not clearly out-of-sample estimates for test sessions. Under a proper temporal split, propensities would be estimated from historical data and applied to future data; under the proposed stratified random split, propensity estimation and evaluation are entangled across the same time window, potentially inflating the apparent effectiveness of bias correction.

The design claims to test an offline surrogate for a live ranking system but does not account for feedback loop effects: The 24-month log was generated by a sequence of production ranking policies that changed over time. Training a new ranker on this data and evaluating it on held-out sessions from the same log conflates the ranker's intrinsic relevance signal with the historical policy's influence on which items users ever saw and clicked. The design acknowledges this partially via IPS but does not discuss counterfactual evaluation methods (e.g., doubly robust estimators or replay-based evaluation) that are standard in off-policy learning from logged feedback.

Statistical significance testing is described only for AUROC, leaving secondary metrics without formal inference: The design specifies bootstrap confidence intervals on the AUROC difference but does not describe significance testing for MRR@10, NDCG@10, or the stratified subgroup results. Given that multiple secondary metrics and multiple stratification cuts are reported, there is a multiple comparisons problem that is not addressed. A result that is marginally significant on the primary metric but significant on several secondary metrics could be misinterpreted as robust evidence when it reflects familywise type-I error inflation.

Shared encoder trained on marketplace text is not clearly defined as in-scope or out-of-scope for the comparison: The transformer uses a BERT-style encoder pre-trained on marketplace text. It is not specified whether this pre-training corpus includes data from the same 24-month window used for the experiment. If pre-training was performed on the full 24-month corpus (or any portion overlapping the test window), the model has indirect access to test-period text distributions, which is a form of leakage that BM25 does not benefit from.
smoke_response.reasoning: The experiment has serious enough methodological flaws to prevent approval in its current form: the random stratified split across 24 months of session data introduces temporal leakage that will produce overoptimistic offline metrics inconsistent with production behavior, and the deliberate feature asymmetry between the transformer and BM25 means a positive result would confirm that click context is useful but would not validate the transformer architecture specifically — directly undermining the stated hypothesis. These two issues alone require redesign; the additional concerns around metric choice, IPS entanglement, and incomplete statistical inference compound the problem.
